pitch.plain: How high or low a sound is.
pitch.definition: The perceived highness or lowness of a sound, set by its vibration frequency — faster vibration sounds higher. On bass, pressing closer to the body raises the pitch; thicker strings sit lower.

note.plain: One named musical sound — like A, C♯, or E♭.
note.definition: A pitch with a name. Music uses twelve note names that repeat as you go higher or lower: A, A♯/B♭, B, C, C♯/D♭, D, D♯/E♭, E, F, F♯/G♭, G, G♯/A♭.

string.plain: The four steel wires you pluck; each is named after the note it plays open.
string.definition: A 4-string bass in standard tuning has strings E–A–D–G, from thickest (lowest) to thinnest (highest). "The A string" means the second-thickest string, which sounds the note A when played open.

open-string.plain: Playing a string without pressing any fret.
open-string.definition: A string plucked while no finger holds it down anywhere. Open strings sound each string's tuned note (E, A, D, or G) and are written as fret 0 in tab.

fret.plain: The metal strips along the neck; each one raises the note by one small step.
fret.definition: The metal bars set into the fingerboard. Pressing a string just behind a fret shortens its vibrating length; each fret raises the pitch by one semitone (half step). "5th fret" means the space just behind the fifth metal bar counting from the headstock.

octave.plain: The same note, higher or lower — like two people singing the same tune, one deeper.
octave.definition: The distance between a note and the next note with the same name (12 semitones). Notes an octave apart vibrate in a 2:1 ratio and sound like "the same note, higher/lower". On bass: two strings up and two frets over.

semitone.plain: One fret's worth of distance — the smallest step in Western music.
semitone.definition: The smallest interval in common Western music: the distance from one fret to the next on the same string. Twelve semitones make an octave.

whole-step.definition: An interval of two semitones — two frets on the same string. Scales are usually described as patterns of whole and half steps.

interval.definition: The gap in pitch between two notes, counted in scale steps and given names like "perfect fifth" or "minor third". Intervals are the units bass lines are built from — on the fretboard each interval is a repeatable shape.

root.plain: The home note a chord or scale is built on — the note that gives it its name.
root.definition: The foundational note of a chord or scale: the C in C major, the A in Am7. The bassist's first job in most music is to sound the root when the chord changes.

scale.plain: A set of notes that belong together, played in order — the palette a song draws from.
scale.definition: An ordered collection of notes spanning an octave, defined by its pattern of whole and half steps (e.g. the major scale: W-W-H-W-W-W-H). Melodies and bass lines are mostly built from a scale's notes.

major-scale.plain: The do-re-mi scale — the sunny-sounding yardstick everything else is measured against.
major-scale.definition: A seven-note scale built W-W-H-W-W-W-H from its root. It defines the key, names the scale degrees (1–7), and is the reference for describing every chord and interval ("a minor third is a flattened 3").

minor-scale.plain: The moodier cousin of the major scale.
minor-scale.definition: Usually means the natural minor scale: W-H-W-W-H-W-W, equivalent to a major scale started from its 6th degree. Variants (harmonic, melodic) alter the 6th and 7th notes for stronger pull back to the root.

relative-minor.plain: Two keys that share the exact same notes — one feels bright, one feels dark.
relative-minor.definition: Every major key has a minor key with the identical notes and key signature, rooted a minor third below it (C major ↔ A minor). Which one you're "in" depends on which note feels like home.

pentatonic.plain: A five-note scale that's almost impossible to make sound wrong.
pentatonic.definition: A scale with five notes per octave. The minor pentatonic (1 ♭3 4 5 ♭7) and major pentatonic (1 2 3 5 6) drop the clash-prone notes of their parent scales, which is why they're the workhorse of rock, blues, and funk lines.

blues-scale.plain: The minor pentatonic plus one deliciously wrong note.
blues-scale.definition: The minor pentatonic with an added ♭5 (1 ♭3 4 ♭5 5 ♭7). That extra note — the "blue note" — is used as a passing sting between 4 and 5 rather than a resting place.

mode.plain: The same seven notes, but a different one feels like home.
mode.definition: A scale made by treating a different degree of a parent scale as the root. The major scale's seven modes (Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian) each have a distinct flavor; Dorian and Mixolydian matter most to bassists.

degree.plain: A note's house number inside its scale — 1 through 7.
degree.definition: The position of a note within a scale, counted from the root: in C major, C=1, D=2, E=3… Degrees let one description work in every key ("play root and 5" works in C, in F♯, anywhere).

tonic.plain: Home base — the note (or chord) where the music feels settled.
tonic.definition: The first degree of the key and the chord built on it (the I chord). Music builds tension by leaving the tonic and resolves it by coming back.

dominant.plain: The 5th degree — the chord that leans hard toward home.
dominant.definition: The fifth scale degree and its chord (V). Dominant chords, especially V7, create the strongest pull back to the tonic; the V→I motion is the engine of most Western harmony (and of the bassist's favorite root moves).

cadence.plain: A musical punctuation mark — how a phrase ends.
cadence.definition: A chord motion that closes a phrase: V→I sounds final (period), IV→I sounds churchy-soft (amen), a phrase parking on V sounds like a comma. Bass root motion is what makes cadences land.

chromatic.plain: Moving fret by fret, using the in-between notes.
chromatic.definition: Motion by semitones, including notes outside the current scale. Chromatic approach notes — landing on a chord tone from one fret above or below — are a signature of walking bass and funk lines.

diatonic.plain: Staying inside the key's own notes.
diatonic.definition: Using only the seven notes of the current key/scale. "The diatonic chords of C major" means the seven chords you can build from C major's notes alone.

enharmonic.plain: Two names for the same fret — like C♯ and D♭.
enharmonic.definition: Notes with different names but identical pitch. Which spelling is correct depends on the key context: the note between C and D is C♯ in D major but D♭ in A♭ major.

accidental.plain: A ♯, ♭, or ♮ stuck in front of a note to bend it away from the key.
accidental.definition: A sharp, flat, or natural sign applied to a single note in the music (as opposed to the key signature at the start). It lasts until the end of the bar.

key.plain: The home scale of a song — which set of notes it lives in.
key.definition: The scale (and its tonic) a piece is built around, e.g. "in G major". The key determines the key signature, the diatonic chords, and where home base is.

key-signature.plain: The sharps or flats parked at the start of every line, so they don't have to be written on each note.
key-signature.definition: The cluster of ♯ or ♭ symbols after the clef that applies for the whole piece: one sharp = G major (or E minor), two flats = B♭ major (or G minor), etc. The circle of fifths organizes them.

circle-of-fifths.plain: A clock face of the twelve keys, where neighbors share almost all their notes.
circle-of-fifths.definition: Arrangement of the 12 keys in perfect-fifth steps (C→G→D→…). Moving clockwise adds a sharp, counter-clockwise adds a flat. Neighboring keys differ by one note, which is why so many songs modulate to them.

chord.plain: Several notes that sound at the same time — the harmony the band plays over.
chord.definition: Three or more notes sounded together, named by their root and quality (C, Cm, Cmaj7…). Bassists rarely play full chords, but every bass line outlines the current chord's notes.

triad.plain: The basic three-note chord: root, third, fifth.
triad.definition: A chord of root + 3rd + 5th. The third sets major (bright) vs minor (dark); the fifth is neutral scaffolding. Four flavors: major, minor, diminished, augmented.

third.plain: The note that makes a chord happy or sad.
third.definition: The interval (and chord tone) two scale steps above the root: a major third (4 semitones) makes a major chord, a minor third (3 semitones) makes a minor chord.

fifth.plain: The strongest, most neutral note after the root — the bassist's best friend.
fifth.definition: The interval seven semitones above the root, present in nearly every chord. Root–fifth alternation is the skeleton of country, rock, and polka bass lines alike. On the fretboard: one string up, two frets over (or the same fret one string down for the fifth below).

seventh-chord.plain: A triad with one more note stacked on — where grown-up harmony starts.
seventh-chord.definition: A four-note chord: root, 3rd, 5th, 7th. The common qualities — maj7 (dreamy), dominant 7 (bluesy, wants to move), m7 (mellow), m7♭5 (tense) — cover most songs you'll ever play.

extension.plain: The optional upper floors of a chord: 9ths, 11ths, 13ths.
extension.definition: Chord tones beyond the 7th, made by continuing to stack thirds. They add color, not function — as a bassist you mostly acknowledge them by NOT stepping on them: the root still works.

inversion.plain: A chord with a note other than its root on the bottom.
inversion.definition: Playing a chord over its 3rd or 5th (written as slash chords: C/E means C major with E in the bass). For bassists, slash chords are direct instructions: play the note after the slash.

arpeggio.plain: A chord played one note at a time.
arpeggio.definition: The notes of a chord played in sequence instead of together. The bread and butter of bass lines: root, third, fifth, (seventh), one after another.

chord-tones.plain: The notes that belong to the chord being played right now.
chord-tones.definition: The current chord's own notes (root, 3rd, 5th, 7th). Landing on chord tones on strong beats is the core recipe for bass lines that sound "right"; everything else is decoration between them.

passing-tone.plain: A stepping-stone note between two chord tones.
passing-tone.definition: A non-chord note used on a weak beat to walk from one chord tone to another by step. Passing tones turn root–root plods into lines.

approach-note.plain: A note played just before your target, one step or one fret away — so the landing feels aimed.
approach-note.definition: A note that leads into a chord tone (usually into the next chord's root) from a scale step or semitone above or below. The last-beat approach note is the signature move of walking bass and countless soul/rock lines.

chord-progression.plain: The sequence of chords a song cycles through.
chord-progression.definition: An ordered chord pattern, usually written with Roman numerals so it works in any key (I–V–vi–IV). The progression is the map a bass line navigates.

roman-numerals.plain: Naming chords by their position in the key — I, IV, V — instead of their letter.
roman-numerals.definition: Writing chords as the scale degree they're built on: uppercase = major (IV), lowercase = minor (ii), ° = diminished. "ii–V–I" describes the same motion in every key, which is why musicians talk this way.

nashville-numbers.plain: The same idea as Roman numerals, but with plain digits — session-musician shorthand.
nashville-numbers.definition: A chart system writing chords as scale-degree numbers (1 4 5, with − for minor: 2−). Made for transposing on the spot: the singer changes key, the chart doesn't change at all.

beat.plain: The steady pulse you tap your foot to.
beat.definition: The regular pulse underlying music. Beats group into bars (in most rock/pop, four beats per bar), and the bass usually locks tightly to them with the drums.

tempo.plain: How fast the music goes — the speed of the beat.
tempo.definition: The speed of the underlying pulse, measured in beats per minute (bpm). 60 bpm is one beat per second; 120 bpm is two. Practice metronomes count it out loud.

bar.plain: One small box of musical time — usually four beats long.
bar.definition: A group of beats, marked off in written music with vertical lines. The time signature says how many beats each bar holds. Charts are read bar by bar.

time-signature.plain: The two stacked numbers that say how beats are grouped — like 4/4.
time-signature.definition: A fraction-looking symbol at the start of written music: the top number is how many beats per bar, the bottom is which note value counts as one beat. 4/4 (four quarter-note beats per bar) is so common it's called "common time".

meter.plain: The repeating pattern of strong and weak beats.
meter.definition: How beats group and which are emphasized: 4/4 goes STRONG-weak-medium-weak; 3/4 goes STRONG-weak-weak; 6/8 rolls in two big pulses of three. Meter is what makes a waltz feel different from a march at the same speed.

downbeat.plain: Downbeats are the numbered foot-taps; offbeats are the "&"s between them.
downbeat.definition: The downbeat is the start of the bar (beat 1) or, loosely, any on-the-beat moment; offbeats are the in-between subdivisions ("1 & 2 &" — the &s). Where you place notes relative to these defines the groove.

subdivision.plain: Slicing each beat into smaller equal pieces — and feeling them even when you don't play them.
subdivision.definition: Dividing beats into halves (eighth notes: "1 & 2 &"), quarters (sixteenths: "1 e & a"), or triplets ("1 trip-let"). Great time comes from hearing the subdivision continuously, not from counting bars.

note-value.plain: How long a note lasts, in beats — whole, half, quarter, eighth, sixteenth.
note-value.definition: The rhythmic length of a written note: in 4/4 a whole note = 4 beats, half = 2, quarter = 1, eighth = ½, sixteenth = ¼. A dot adds half the note's own length again.

rest.plain: Written silence — a note you play by NOT playing.
rest.definition: A symbol marking measured silence, with the same length system as notes. For bassists rests are active: stopping a string on time (muting) is as much technique as sounding it.

tie.plain: A curved line gluing two notes into one longer note.
tie.definition: A curve connecting two notes of the same pitch: play once, hold for their combined length. Used to carry notes across beats or bar lines. (A curve between different pitches is a slur — play smoothly.)

syncopation.plain: Putting notes where the beat isn't — the rhythmic surprise that makes you move.
syncopation.definition: Accenting offbeats or skipping expected downbeats. Funk, reggae, and Latin bass live on syncopation; the trick is feeling the steady subdivision underneath so the "wrong" placements are exact.

swing.plain: Playing pairs of notes long-short instead of evenly — the skip in blues and jazz.
swing.definition: A feel where each beat's two eighth notes are uneven: the first stretched, the second compressed (roughly a triplet's long+short). "Straight" means literal, even eighths. Same notes, completely different feel.

groove.plain: The rhythmic feel that makes people nod their heads.
groove.definition: The repeating rhythmic pattern and feel of a piece — where notes land, how long they last, which ones are stressed. For a bassist, playing "in the pocket" (locked with the drums, steady and intentional) matters more than playing many notes.

ghost-note.plain: A muted "thunk" with rhythm but almost no pitch — the percussion inside a bass line.
ghost-note.definition: A note played with the fretting hand only damping the string (written as an ×). Ghost notes fill the spaces between real notes and are the secret sauce of funk and R&B bass.

sixteenth-groove.plain: Grooves built on four slices per beat — funk's home turf.
sixteenth-groove.definition: Bass lines whose rhythmic grid is sixteenth notes ("1 e & a"). Few of the sixteen slots are actually played; the pattern of hits, rests, and ghost notes is the groove.

dynamics.plain: How loud or soft you play — and the difference between the two.
dynamics.definition: The volume dimension of music, marked p (soft) through f (loud), with hairpins for gradual change. On bass, dynamics come from pluck strength and are half of what people call "feel".

accent.plain: Hitting one note harder so it pokes out of the line.
accent.definition: Emphasis on a single note (written >). Consistent accent placement — beat 1, or the backbeat, or a syncopated hit — is a big part of what defines a style's feel.

staccato.plain: Short, separated notes vs. smooth, connected ones.
staccato.definition: Staccato (a dot over the note): cut the note short and leave air. Legato (a slur line): connect notes with no gap. The same line played staccato or legato grooves completely differently — note length is a bassist's superpower.

metronome.plain: A device that clicks a perfectly steady beat for you to practice with.
metronome.definition: A tool that sounds the beat at an exact tempo. Practicing with one builds the timing that bandmates rely on from a bassist. This site's labs include one, and practice exercises give metronome targets.

tab.plain: Bass-specific notation: four lines for the four strings, numbers for the frets.
tab.definition: Short for tablature. Each horizontal line is a string (bottom line = low E), each number is a fret to press (0 = open string). Tab shows you where; standard notation shows you what and when — this site teaches both, side by side.

tuning.plain: Adjusting each string until it sounds its correct note.
tuning.definition: Setting each string's pitch with the tuning machines on the headstock. Standard bass tuning is E1–A1–D2–G2, low to high. Basses drift out of tune with temperature and playing — checking takes seconds with a tuner.

intonation.plain: Whether the bass is still in tune higher up the neck, not just on the open strings.
intonation.definition: How accurately pitch tracks along the whole string length. If open strings are in tune but the 12th fret is sharp or flat, the bridge saddles need adjusting — a standard part of a setup.

action.plain: How high the strings float above the frets.
action.definition: String height over the fingerboard. Lower action = easier to press, at the risk of buzz; higher action = more effort, cleaner ring. Set at the bridge (and nut) as part of a setup — worth paying a tech once to get right.

pickup.plain: The magnetic bar under the strings that turns their motion into your signal.
pickup.definition: A magnet-and-coil sensor under the strings. Plucking closer to the bridge pickup gives a tight, mid-heavy bite; closer to the neck gives a rounder, deeper sound — position of your plucking hand matters as much as the knobs.

tone.plain: The character of your sound — round, bright, punchy, dull — before anyone asks how loud.
tone.definition: The timbral quality of your notes. On bass it's controlled by where and how you pluck, fretting cleanliness, the tone knob (rolls off treble), pickup choice, and note length — mostly hands, only partly gear.

fingerstyle.plain: Plucking with your index and middle fingers, alternating like walking.
fingerstyle.definition: The standard bass plucking technique: fingers alternate strictly (i-m-i-m), striking through the string to rest on the next one (rest stroke). Even alternation at slow tempos is the foundation everything fast is built on.

rest-stroke.plain: Plucking through the string so your finger lands on the next string up.
rest-stroke.definition: A pluck that follows through and comes to rest on the adjacent (thicker) string. It gives a fatter tone and automatically mutes the string you land on — two jobs, one motion.

raking.plain: Letting one finger fall across two or three strings in a row instead of re-plucking.
raking.definition: When crossing to a lower string, dragging the same finger across strings in one motion. Economical and smooth — a standard part of fingerstyle descending lines.

muting.plain: Keeping every string quiet except the one that's supposed to be talking.
muting.definition: Damping unwanted string noise with both hands: fretting-hand touch, plucking-hand thumb or palm on idle strings, rest strokes. Muting is what makes a bass line sound tight instead of muddy — it's not optional, it IS the technique.

one-finger-per-fret.plain: Assigning your four fingers to four neighboring frets so nothing requires a jump.
one-finger-per-fret.definition: The default fretting-hand system: index, middle, ring, and pinky each own a fret in the current position. It minimizes movement and makes fingerings consistent — with a low-position relaxation (index covering two frets) when stretches are big near the nut.

position.plain: Where your hand is parked on the neck — named by the fret under your index finger.
position.definition: A four-fret window the fretting hand covers without moving: "2nd position" = index on fret 2. Playing "in position" means finding the notes you need inside the window instead of running up and down one string.

shifting.plain: Moving your hand to a new spot on the neck — smoothly, on purpose.
shifting.definition: Relocating the fretting hand to a new position. Good shifts are planned (usually on open strings, rests, or long notes), use a light thumb, and land with the finger already over its target fret.

hammer-on.plain: Sounding a second note with the fretting hand alone — no pluck.
hammer-on.definition: Hammer-on: snap a finger down onto a higher fret so the note sounds (written h). Pull-off: flick a finger off to sound a lower fretted note (written p). Together they make fast, smooth (legato) passages possible.

slide.plain: Gliding a pressed finger along the string from one note to another.
slide.definition: Keeping pressure while moving along the string so the pitch sweeps (written /). Slides connect positions and add vocal-style expression to lines.

slap.plain: Thumb-whack the low strings, snap the high ones — bass as a drum kit.
slap.definition: A percussive technique: the thumb strikes the string against the frets (slap) and fingers yank the higher strings so they snap back (pop), usually woven with ghost notes. The defining sound of a lot of funk.

palm-mute.plain: Resting the side of your plucking palm on the strings for a thumpy, old-school sound.
palm-mute.definition: Damping the strings near the bridge with the edge of the plucking hand (often with thumb plucking). Shortens sustain into a warm thump — think Motown or reggae flavors.

bass-line.plain: What the bassist actually plays: the low melody that glues rhythm to harmony.
bass-line.definition: The bass part of a song — a line that states the harmony (mostly chord tones, roots on the changes) while locking rhythmically with the drums. The best ones are singable.

walking-bass.plain: A steady note on every beat that strolls through the chord changes.
walking-bass.definition: The jazz/blues style of playing quarter notes continuously, outlining each chord and aiming an approach note at each change. Built from chord tones, scale steps, and chromatic connectors.

twelve-bar-blues.plain: The most-played chord progression on Earth — twelve bars of I, IV, and V.
twelve-bar-blues.definition: A repeating 12-bar form: 4 bars of I, 2 of IV, 2 of I, then V–IV–I(–V turnaround). Thousands of songs use it verbatim; every bassist needs it on autopilot in several keys.

turnaround.plain: The last couple of bars that whip the music back to the top of the form.
turnaround.definition: A short progression (often ending on V) at the end of a section that sets up the repeat. Bass fills live here — it's the spot where a little motion is expected.

form.plain: The song's floor plan — verse, chorus, bridge, and how they repeat.
form.definition: The large-scale structure of a piece (12-bar blues, 32-bar AABA, verse–chorus). Bassists navigate by form: knowing where you are in it matters more than any single note.

chart.plain: The one-page cheat sheet of a song: chords, bars, and the road map.
chart.definition: A written summary of a song — chord symbols over slashes or bars, with repeats, endings, and section letters. Reading charts (rather than full notation) is how most working bands communicate.

vamp.plain: Looping one or two chords until further notice.
vamp.definition: A short repeated progression (often a single chord) held for an open-ended stretch — the natural habitat of groove playing and a great practice format.

fill.plain: A little extra flurry that decorates the space between phrases.
fill.definition: A brief melodic figure a bassist adds at phrase ends or turnarounds. Good fills start and end on time, target the next chord's root, and never sink the groove.

improvisation.plain: Making the line up as you go — inside the song's rules.
improvisation.definition: Spontaneously creating parts from the chord progression, the style's vocabulary, and your ear. For bassists it usually means varying grooves and fills, not soloing — though that's here too.

ear-training.plain: Teaching your ears to name what they hear — intervals, chords, and lines.
ear-training.definition: Deliberate practice recognizing intervals, chord qualities, and progressions by sound. For bassists it's the bridge between hearing a song and playing it without sheet music.

transcription.plain: Lifting a bass line off a recording by ear, note by note.
transcription.definition: Working out (and optionally writing down) exactly what was played on a record. The single highest-value practice habit for intermediate players: it trains ears, hands, vocabulary, and feel simultaneously.

riff.plain: A short, repeated musical phrase — the hook a song is built on.
riff.definition: A short repeating figure, often played by bass and guitar together, that defines a song's identity. Learning riffs trains both hands and your ear at once.
